questions.question: Explain the concept of Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) and distinguish between hard and soft real-time systems with examples.
keyPoints: RTOS guarantees response within time constraints
Hard real-time: missing deadline = system failure (e.g., pacemakers, aircraft control)
Soft real-time: missing deadline degrades performance (e.g., video streaming)
Predictable timing is the core requirement

questions.question: What are the key characteristics of embedded operating systems? Give two examples of devices that use embedded OS.
keyPoints: Designed for specific dedicated functions
Lightweight with minimal footprint
Limited user interface
Low power consumption
Examples: microwave ovens, washing machines, ATMs, automotive control units

questions.question: Differentiate between Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP) and Asymmetric Multiprocessing (AMP).
keyPoints: SMP: all processors identical, share memory, OS runs on any processor, load balanced
AMP: master-slave relationship, specific tasks assigned to specific processors
SMP provides better resource utilization
AMP is simpler to implement and debug

questions.question: What is Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)? State its assumptions.
keyPoints: Fixed priority scheduling algorithm
Higher priority given to tasks with shorter periods
Assumptions: all tasks periodic, deadlines equal periods, no task dependencies, context switch time negligible

questions.question: Explain the working of Earliest Deadline First (EDF) scheduling algorithm with an example.
keyPoints: Dynamic priority scheduling algorithm
Priorities calculated at each scheduling decision based on deadline
Task with earliest deadline gets highest priority
More optimal than RMS but harder to implement predictably

questions.question: What is a distributed operating system? How does it differ from a network operating system?
keyPoints: Manages multiple computers as single system
Users see unified file system and processes
Network OS: computers remain independent, users explicitly access network resources
Distributed OS provides transparency

questions.question: Discuss the design considerations for operating systems used in Internet of Things (IoT) devices.
keyPoints: Extremely limited memory and processing power
Must support low-power operation and sleep modes
Minimal communication protocols
Security with limited resources
Real-time capabilities for monitoring applications

questions.question: Explain the need for special-purpose operating systems. Compare them with general-purpose operating systems highlighting their distinguishing features.
keyPoints: Special-purpose OS tailored for specific domains
General-purpose handles diverse applications
Comparison: Resource constraints, customization, reliability, response time, hardware dependency
Examples of special-purpose: RTOS, embedded, network, multiprocessor OS

questions.question: Describe the various types of special-purpose operating systems in detail with suitable examples of each type.
keyPoints: Real-time OS (hard and soft) - used in control systems
Embedded OS - devices like microwaves, cars
Distributed OS - manages networked computers
Multiprocessor OS - parallel processing systems
Network OS - server management
Handheld OS - smartphones/tablets
Sensor OS - TinyOS for wireless sensors

questions.question: What is multiprocessor operating system? Explain the different multiprocessor system architectures with suitable diagrams description.
keyPoints: OS designed for systems with multiple processors
Tight-coupling vs loose-coupling architectures
SMP: shared memory, all processors equal
AMP: master-slave model
NUMA: non-uniform memory access
Cluster computing: multiple independent computers networked together

questions.question: Explain the concept of priority inversion and how it affects real-time systems. Discuss methods to prevent priority inversion.
keyPoints: Priority inversion: lower priority task blocks higher priority task
Caused by resource sharing with mutexes
Example: Task L (low) holds lock, Task H (high) waits, Task M (medium) preempts L
Solutions: Priority Inheritance Protocol, Priority Ceiling Protocol
Breaks chain of blocking by temporarily boosting priority
